lead: A Stowmarket moving day runs far more smoothly when there is a clear plan behind it, and after years of carrying homes in and out of this central Suffolk town we have learned exactly where the pinch points tend to be. This checklist is the one we wish every customer had pinned to the fridge the week before we arrive. It walks you through the run-up, the morning itself and the first night in your new Stowmarket home, with the small practical details that turn a stressful day into a calm one.

sections.h: Two Weeks Before: Get the Big Jobs Booked
sections.body:
The fortnight before moving day is when the groundwork gets done, and in Stowmarket the biggest early job is usually sorting access. If your new home is on one of the newer estates around the edge of town, check whether there are any temporary parking restrictions while building work continues nearby, and let us know. If you are moving into one of the older terraces near the centre or the station, take a proper look at where a removal lorry can realistically stop. Streets that look fine for a car can be tight for a long vehicle, and a quick word with a neighbour in advance often frees up the space we need.

This is also the time to confirm your moving date with us and to start running down the freezer and the food cupboards. Notify the important people of your change of address, arrange for mail redirection, and book any time off work you will need. If your chain is looking shaky, talk to us early about storage; we would far rather plan a short-term solution calmly now than scramble on the day if completion slips. Stowmarket completions, like everywhere, do not always land neatly, so having a fallback removes a lot of worry.

Start gathering packing materials too, or ask us about supplying them. Decent boxes, tape, bubble wrap and marker pens make a real difference, and running out halfway through packing is a genuine morale killer. Aim to have everything you need in the house before you begin in earnest, so the packing flows rather than stalling every time you reach for materials that are not there.

sections.h: One Week Before: Pack Room by Room
sections.body:
With seven days to go, packing should be in full swing. The trick we always pass on is to work room by room rather than dotting about the house, and to start with the rooms you use least. The spare room, the loft and the garage are the obvious early targets. The loft in particular catches people out; it holds far more than anyone remembers, and discovering a dozen heavy boxes up a ladder on moving morning throws the whole day out. Clear it this week, not on the day.

Label every box clearly on the side, not just the top, with the room it belongs to in your new Stowmarket home and a quick note of what is inside. We carry boxes straight to the right room as we unload, so good labels mean your things end up where they belong rather than piled in the hallway. A colour-coding system works brilliantly: a colour per room, matching stickers on the boxes and a matching sheet on each door at the new house. Even a crew seeing your home for the first time can then unload at speed.

Keep anything precious, important documents, jewellery, small valuables, set aside to travel in your own car. Mark fragile boxes on more than one side. And as you pack, keep a simple numbered list of what is in each box; if any part of your move involves storage or a two-day handover, that inventory turns the unpacking from a treasure hunt into something manageable. By the end of this week, only the kitchen essentials and the beds should be left.

The day before the move is about tying off loose ends. Defrost and empty the fridge and freezer, strip all but the beds you will sleep in tonight, and finish the last of the packing so that when we arrive in the morning everything is sealed and ready except the kettle and the breakfast things. The single biggest cause of a slow, expensive moving day is packing that is not finished when the crew turns up; the more boxed and ready you are, the faster and calmer the day runs.

Take meter readings at the old property and note them down with the date. Charge your phone, and pack your essentials box, the most important box of the lot. Into it goes the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents, and a few toys if you have little ones. This box rides in your own car, never on the lorry, so you can reach everything the moment you arrive without unpacking the whole van.

Have a think about the morning logistics too. Clear a path from the front door to the kerb at both ends if you can. If you have young children or pets, arrange for someone to look after them for the day, or set up one already-cleared room as their safe base. Stowmarket can get busy around the station and on market days, so if your route in town passes either, a little timing thought tonight saves frustration tomorrow.

sections.h: Moving Morning: Working With the Crew
sections.body:
On the morning itself, the most useful thing you can do is be ready and be present. Open the curtains, switch on the lights so we can see, and make sure nothing is still hidden in the loft, the shed or behind a door. When we arrive we like a quick walk-round so you can point out the fragile boxes, the antiques, the artwork and anything that needs extra care. We pad and load those thoughtfully, but we can only do that if we know which items they are.

Leave out a kettle, mugs and a few biscuits if you can; it keeps the team going and it is the small courtesy our crews always remember. Keep children and pets in their safe room with a clear note on the door so nobody opens it by mistake, especially as the front door will stand open for much of the morning. Cats in particular have a habit of bolting the moment the chance arises, so tell us where they are and we will all keep that door shut.

Before we drive away, do a proper walk-through of the old place together. Check every cupboard, the loft, the shed, the garden and behind every door, because it is astonishing what gets left in the rush. Confirm the inventory, hand over or arrange the old keys, and make sure you have the keys for your new Stowmarket home. Then it is simply a case of meeting us at the other end, where we will unload to the right rooms and rebuild the beds so you have somewhere comfortable to collapse that night.

sections.h: Arriving in Stowmarket: The First Hours
sections.body:
Arriving at the new house is where good labelling pays off. As we unload, we carry each box to the room marked on its side, so within a couple of hours your home has structure rather than being one enormous pile in the hall. Point us to where the big furniture goes before we bring it in; it is far easier to place a wardrobe correctly first time than to shuffle it across a room once it is full. If you have colour-coded the doors, the whole unload moves faster and with far fewer questions.

Get the kitchen working enough for a brew and a simple meal, and ask us to make up the beds before we leave. After a long day, the difference between a made-up bed and a mattress buried under boxes is the difference between a good first night in Stowmarket and a miserable one. Find your essentials box, put the kettle on, and resist the urge to unpack everything tonight. The boxes will still be there tomorrow, and a proper rest is the best possible start.

Once you are settled, take the meter readings at the new property and note them, locate the stopcock and the consumer unit, and have a quick check that everything has arrived safely. Over the coming days you can unpack at a sensible pace, room by room, and start to enjoy the town. A walk to the high street, a first trip to the Food Museum with the children, a coffee while you find your bearings; these small outings are how a new house starts to feel like home. We will have done our part by then, and every load we carry is fully covered, so you can settle in knowing the hard part is behind you.

faqs: When should I start packing for my Stowmarket move?

Begin around two weeks out with the rooms you use least, such as the spare room, loft and garage, and work room by room from there. Aim to have everything except the kettle and the beds packed by the day before, so the morning crew can load straight away.
What should I check about access before moving day in Stowmarket?

Look at where a removal lorry can realistically stop at both properties. Newer Stowmarket estates may have temporary parking restrictions during building work, while older central terraces and streets near the station can be tight. Tell us in advance and we bring the right vehicle.
What goes in my moving day essentials box?

Pack the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys for children. Keep it in your own car rather than on the lorry so it is to hand the moment you reach your new Stowmarket home.
Will you rebuild our beds when we arrive in Stowmarket?

Yes. We can dismantle beds at the old property and reassemble them at the new one before we leave. After a long moving day, having beds ready to sleep in makes a real difference to your first night in Stowmarket, so just let us know which ones you want made up.
